title: The long game is distributed mixture of experts at the SOTA level plus a data flywheel of dynamically-trained specialized subscribers
content: The long-game vision for AMS is captured in HORIZON §13 as three layered shifts. (1) SOTA-level mixture of experts across providers: per role, per function, per modality, per analysis type, the best-in-class model subscribes to the conversation; routing happens per-subtask rather than per-session. (2) Conversations as training corpus for specialized models: with customer data-governance permission, the conversation history trains HRMs (Hierarchical Reasoning Models), domain classifiers, and in-house reasoners that subsequently subscribe to those same conversations as additional experts — a flywheel where data trains specialists, specialists contribute to conversations, contributions become future training data. (3) A three-tier subscriber stack composed dynamically per turn: frontier (open-ended reasoning), specialized (in-distribution domain tasks), deterministic (MCP servers and direct integrations for known-answer operations). The wire carries; the harness decides which tier handles which subtask. Event-driven orchestration (§3.7) is step one toward this; distributed MoE across providers is step two; the data flywheel producing continuously-improving specialized subscribers is step three.

title: The composition primitive that frontier labs use internally becomes available on an open substrate is the structural democratization claim
content: The strategic claim is sharper than "multi-agent infrastructure." It is: AMS makes available, on an open substrate across providers and organizations, the same composition primitive that OpenAI/Anthropic/Google/xAI use behind their walled-garden APIs. Not "everyone gets a frontier model" (they don't). But "everyone gets the composition substrate that makes a frontier model just one ingredient among several." The strategic moat shifts from "who has the biggest model" to "who has the best composition discipline and the right specialized subscribers for their domain." This is the structural democratization argument and is the framing for the long-game pitch.

title: PoC scope is forward-compatible with §13 long-game vision — no foreclosure detected
content: Per the constraint-half framing established for HORIZON, every catalog entry is a forward-compatibility check on shipped decisions. Audit of PoC scope (per SPEC.md) against §13 long game: (1) multi-provider routing lives in the harness above AMS, no wire-level foreclosure; (2) per-subtask model selection uses capability metadata which is already in PROTOCOL.md §4.4; (3) specialized model subscribers are just polymorphic subscribers per AMS.md §5; (4) conversation-as-training-corpus requires conversation persistence (deferred but not foreclosed in SPEC.md §5); (5) dynamic model spawning maps to the wake-up surface in AMS.md §3.3; (6) three-tier routing is a harness concern; (7) customer data governance can be declared in conversation metadata. PoC ships against the long game without painting corners.

title: Distributed mixture of experts, three-tier subscriber stack, data flywheel
content: Distributed mixture of experts: the cross-provider, cross-organization composition primitive — per-subtask SOTA model selection happening on AMS as the substrate rather than inside any one provider's walls. Three-tier subscriber stack: frontier (SOTA general-purpose), specialized (small models trained on the conversation corpus, dynamically spawned), deterministic (MCP servers and APIs for known-answer operations) — composed per turn per subtask on a single conversation. Data flywheel: conversations generate data, data trains specialists, specialists subscribe and contribute, contributions become future training data — the substrate continuously improves the specialized tier without human re-engineering.
